mappings.rationaleAI: The French Revolution of 1789, the Third Estate revolt, the Jacobins, the Terror, and the First Republic are central topics (A.1.b).
Napoleon's rise, the coalitions against France, the Continental System, and defeat at Waterloo are extensively covered (A.1.b-c).
Napoleon's career as first consul and emperor, the Code Napoleon, and his military campaigns are central to A.1.b.iii-iv and A.1.c.
The Industrial Revolution's development from British origins through Continental spread and the factory system is explicitly discussed in A.2.
World War I, including the Serbian crisis, trench warfare, new military technology, and the armistice, is covered in detail (C).
The Paris Peace Conference, German reparations, reorganization of central Europe, and the League of Nations are discussed (C.3).
The 1917 Russian Revolution, abolition of the monarchy, and the Treaty of Brest-Litovsk are covered in the WWI section (C.2).
Russia from Alexander I through Nicholas II, including serfdom's abolition, revolutionary movements, and the Russo-Japanese War (B.2).
The Austrian and Austro-Hungarian Empire, including the Ausgleich, Dual Monarchy, and its dissolution in 1918, is covered in B.1 and B.2.
Cultural nationalism, the Risorgimento, German unification under Bismarck, and national movements in the Habsburg and Ottoman empires are pervasive themes.
The social consequences of industrialization, the division between capitalist and worker, and the rise of socialist movements connect to Marx's thought (A.2).
Socialist thought, the Second Republic in France, Fabian socialism, and the growth of the Labour movement in Britain are discussed (A.2, B.1).
The Paris Commune, Social Democrats in Russia and Germany, and revolutionary movements culminating in the 1917 Revolution are relevant (B.1, C.2).
Parliamentary reform, universal suffrage movements, Reform Bills in Britain, and constitutional developments across Europe are major themes (B.1).
The French Revolution, the revolutions of 1830 and 1848, and the Russian Revolution of 1917 are all covered (A.1, A.2, C.2).
The resurgence of European imperialism from c. 1875-1914, the scramble for Africa, and the partitioning of Asia are discussed (B.3).

mappings.vsiAuthor: Christopher Harvie, H. C. G. Matthew
mappings.rationaleAI: Mid-Victorian politics, Gladstone's liberalism, Disraeli's imperialism, the Irish Question, and social reform movements are covered (B.1).
France from the Revolution through the Third Republic, including Napoleon III, the Commune, and the Dreyfus affair, is covered (A.1, B.1-B.2).
Italian unification under Cavour and Garibaldi and Italian participation in WWI are covered (B.1, B.2).
The Romantic movement as an intellectual and artistic development of the period is explicitly discussed (A.2).
Gladstone's liberalism, free trade, and liberal constitutionalism across Europe are recurring themes (B.1, B.2).
The Napoleonic Wars and the development of modern military strategy are the direct context for Clausewitz's theories of warfare (A.1.c).
German Idealism including Fichte, Hegel, and Schopenhauer is explicitly discussed as a philosophical development of this period (A.2).

Schopenhauer is explicitly named in the outline alongside Kant, Fichte, and Hegel as a key figure in the philosophical developments of this period (A.2.d.iii: 'the role of Immanuel Kant and his disciples (Fichte, Hegel, and Schopenhauer), German Idealism').
Kant and his disciples are explicitly identified as central to the philosophical developments of this period (A.2).
The principle of evolution is explicitly mentioned as a new scientific development affecting society during this period (B.3).

Einsteinian relativity is explicitly named in the outline as a major scientific development of this period (B.3.d.i: 'Einsteinian relativity, the social effects of medical advances, development of the behavioral sciences, new views of the universe').
Bismarck's alliance system, the Triple Entente, and the prelude to WWI are major diplomatic themes (B.2, C.1).
Industrialization, the division between capitalist and worker, and economic transformations of the 19th century are central (A.2, B.1).
Industrialization from 1870-1914, the changing balance of economic power, and the revolution in transportation are covered (A.2, B.3).
Total war, mobilization of populations, new military technology including air power, tanks, and poison gas in WWI are discussed (C.1-C.2).
The outline explicitly discusses 'the leadership, industrial strength, strategic plans and goals, and tactical and logistical procedures of the belligerents' in WWI (C.2.b).
Air power is explicitly mentioned as a new military technology introduced during World War I (C.1).
Bismarck's social legislation, Lloyd George's National Insurance Act, and factory acts represent early welfare state developments (B.1, B.2).
The decline of aristocratic power, the conflict between nobility and bourgeoisie, and the revolutions of 1848 transformed European hierarchies (A.1, A.2).
The Irish Question, Fenianism, Parnell, Home Rule, and the Easter Rising of 1916 are explicitly discussed (B.1).
The Great Famine, O'Connell, the Young Ireland movement, Home Rule, and the Easter Rising are covered (A.2, B.1).
Tocqueville's analysis of democracy and revolution emerged from the post-revolutionary French context covered in A.1-A.2.
The social consequences of the Industrial Revolution and the rise of socialism connect to Engels's contributions (A.2).
Victorian social attitudes, liberalism, the High Church movement, and mid-Victorian politics and economics are discussed (B.1).
New military technology including air power, tanks, and poison gas, and the revolution in transportation are discussed (C.1).
British imperial expansion, the South African War, and British gains in the WWI peace settlements are covered (B.3, C.3).
Scottish developments including Edinburgh's intellectual prominence and the revival of Scottish culture are referenced (A.2, B.1).
The European scramble for Africa and partitioning of Asia from c. 1875-1914 set the stage for later decolonization (B.3).
Militant nationalism, dissolution of empires in WWI, and social dislocations of industrialization provided conditions that gave rise to fascism (B.2, C).
Jewish emancipation and the development of national consciousness among European peoples provide context for the emergence of Zionism (B.1, B.2).
